schools.summary: The LORD speaking in mount Sinai concerning the sabbath of the land: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses in mount Sinai, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses in mount Sinai, now to give the law of the sabbatical year and the jubilee (ch. 25, the in mount Sinai, the place of the giving, marking the solemnity, perhaps that this law was given at Sinai though to be kept in the land, the occasion, after the feasts of the LORD, now the sabbath of the land and the year of liberty, the rest and release that crown the sacred calendar, this law given at Sinai, the mount of the covenant, marking its weight, the sabbath of the land and the jubilee resting on God's covenant authority); the LORD speaking in mount Sinai (the law of the sabbatical year) — the solemnity (the place of the covenant); the LORD spake unto Moses — the law of the land's sabbath introduced (the providence, God giving the law at Sinai, the covenant authority) — Henry, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses in mount Sinai, saying
the LORD speaking in mount Sinai concerning the sabbath of the land
God giving the law of the sabbatical year and the jubilee (ch. 25)
resting on the covenant authority of Sinai

schools.summary: The land keeping a sabbath unto the LORD: 'speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, When ye come into the land which I give you, then shall the land keep a sabbath unto the LORD' — when Israel entered the land, the land itself to keep a sabbath unto the LORD (the seventh-year rest, the when ye come into the land, the law to be kept in Canaan, the the land keep a sabbath unto the LORD, the land's rest, the seventh year, a sabbath to God, the figure, the sabbath rest extended to the land, the land resting unto the LORD, the principle of the sabbath reaching beyond man to the very ground, the rest that honors God as Lord of the land, the trust that lets the land lie fallow at his command, Exodus 23:10-11); the land given by God (the law for the land) — the gift (the land God's to command); 'then shall the land keep a sabbath unto the LORD' (the land's seventh-year rest, a sabbath to God, Exodus 23:10-11, the figure, the sabbath reaching the land, the rest unto the LORD, the providence, the land's sabbath, the trust in God's provision, vv.3-7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: When ye come into the land which I give you, then shall the land keep a sabbath unto the LORD
the land keeping a sabbath unto the LORD
the sabbath rest extended to the land, the land resting unto the LORD
the trust that lets the land lie fallow at his command (Exodus 23:10-11)

schools.summary: The six years of sowing and gathering: 'six years thou shalt sow thy field, and six years thou shalt prune thy vineyard, and gather in the fruit thereof' — for six years the land tilled, sown, pruned, and its fruit gathered, before the seventh-year rest (the six years thou shalt sow, the tilling of the field, the prune thy vineyard, the dressing of the vine, the gather in the fruit, the harvest, the figure, the six years of labor before the seventh of rest, as the six days of work before the sabbath, Exodus 20:9, the rhythm of work and rest woven into the land as into the week, the six of labor and the seventh of rest); the six years of tilling (the field sown) — the labor (the six years of work); 'six years thou shalt prune thy vineyard, and gather in the fruit thereof' (the pruning and gathering, the six years of labor and harvest, Exodus 20:9, the figure, the six of work before the seventh of rest, the providence, the six years of labor, the seventh of rest, vv.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Six years thou shalt sow thy field, and six years thou shalt prune thy vineyard, and gather in the fruit thereof
the six years of sowing and gathering
the six years of labor before the seventh of rest, as the six days before the sabbath (Exodus 20:9)
the rhythm of work and rest woven into the land as into the week

schools.summary: The seventh-year sabbath of rest for the land: 'but in the seventh year shall be a sabbath of rest unto the land, a sabbath for the LORD: thou shalt neither sow thy field, nor prune thy vineyard' — the land's sabbath — 'in the seventh year shall be a sabbath of rest unto the land, a sabbath for the LORD': the seventh year a sabbath of rest for the land, in which there was no sowing or pruning (the witnesses noting the significance — the complete rest of the land every seventh year, the field unsown, the vineyard unpruned, the land lying fallow a whole year, a sabbath to the LORD, the lesson — the trust in God's provision that this rest required, to leave the land untilled a whole year, depending on God to provide, the great exercise of faith, the rest that honors God as the true owner and provider, 'the earth is the LORD's, and the fulness thereof,' Psalm 24:1, the wider truth — the sabbath principle extended to the land, the rest that the very ground enjoys, a figure of the rest of faith and of the great sabbath that remains for the people of God, 'there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God,' Hebrews 4:9, the warning that Israel's neglect of these land-sabbaths brought the seventy years' exile, that the land might at last enjoy her sabbaths, 2 Chronicles 36:21, the figure — the land's sabbath as a call to trust God's provision and a type of the rest that remains, the ceasing from our own works to rest in him); the seventh-year rest of the land (the sabbath to the LORD, Psalm 24:1) — the rest (the land's complete sabbath); 'thou shalt neither sow thy field, nor prune thy vineyard' (the field unsown and vine unpruned, the land fallow, the trust in God's provision, Hebrews 4:9, the figure, the rest of faith, the providence, the land at rest, the trust required) — Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the seventh-year sabbath of the land teach?

sides.position: The complete rest of the land every seventh year, the great exercise of faith that left the land fallow in trust of God's provision (Psalm 24:1) - a figure of the rest of faith and of the sabbath that remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9), the neglect of which brought the exile that the land might enjoy her sabbaths (2 Chronicles 36:21)

themes: But in the seventh year shall be a sabbath of rest unto the land, a sabbath for the LORD: thou shalt neither sow thy field, nor prune thy vineyard
the seventh-year sabbath of rest for the land (the crux of the land's rest and trust in God)
the complete rest of the land, the great exercise of faith in God's provision (Psalm 24:1)
a figure of the rest that remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9; 2 Chronicles 36:21)

schools.summary: The self-grown produce not to be harvested: 'that which groweth of its own accord of thy harvest thou shalt not reap, neither gather the grapes of thy vine undressed: for it is a year of rest unto the land' — in the sabbath year, what grew of itself not to be reaped as a harvest nor the grapes gathered as a vintage, for it was the land's year of rest (the groweth of its own accord, the self-sown produce, the aftergrowth, the not reap... nor gather... undressed, not as a regular harvest or vintage, the it is a year of rest unto the land, the sabbath rest, the figure, the land's rest honored, the self-grown left free, not claimed as a harvest, the ceasing from the ordinary labor and gain, the rest that does not grasp at the land's produce but lets it lie open, vv.6); the self-sown produce not reaped as a harvest (the land's rest honored) — the rest (the harvest not claimed); 'neither gather the grapes of thy vine undressed: for it is a year of rest unto the land' (the grapes not gathered as a vintage, the land's rest, the figure, the ceasing from the ordinary gain, the providence, the land's rest, the produce free to all, vv.6-7) — JFB, Gill.

themes: That which groweth of its own accord of thy harvest thou shalt not reap, neither gather the grapes of thy vine undressed: for it is a year of rest unto the land
the self-grown produce not to be harvested
the land's rest honored, the self-grown left free, not claimed as a harvest
the ceasing from the ordinary labor and gain

schools.summary: The sabbath-year produce as food for all alike: 'and the sabbath of the land shall be meat for you; for thee, and for thy servant, and for thy maid, and for thy hired servant, and for thy stranger that sojourneth with thee' — what the land brought forth of itself in the sabbath year was food for all alike: the owner, the servants, the hired hand, and the stranger (the sabbath of the land shall be meat for you, the produce as food, the for thee, and for thy servant... and for thy stranger, all sharing alike, the figure, the produce of the sabbath year open to all, the leveling of master and servant and stranger, all sharing the land's free gift, the figure of the common provision, the equality before God's bounty, none claiming the harvest as his own, all fed alike from the land's free yield); the self-grown produce as food (the land's free yield) — the provision (the produce for all); 'for thee, and for thy servant... and for thy stranger that sojourneth with thee' (all sharing alike, master, servant, stranger, the figure, the leveling of all before the land's free gift, the providence, the common provision, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the sabbath of the land shall be meat for you; for thee, and for thy servant, and for thy maid, and for thy hired servant, and for thy stranger that sojourneth with thee
the sabbath-year produce as food for all alike
the produce of the sabbath year open to all, the leveling of master and servant and stranger
the equality before God's bounty, all fed alike from the land's free yield

schools.summary: The sabbath-year produce as food even for the cattle and beasts: 'and for thy cattle, and for the beast that are in thy land, shall all the increase thereof be meat' — the increase of the sabbath year was food even for the cattle and the wild beasts of the land (the for thy cattle, the domestic animals, the for the beast that are in thy land, the wild beasts, the all the increase thereof be meat, the produce as food for the creatures too, the figure, the bounty reaching even to the cattle and the wild beasts, God's provision for all his creatures, the figure of the universal provision, the very beasts fed from the land's free yield, the kindness of God reaching to all that he has made, 'the eyes of all wait upon thee... thou openest thine hand, and satisfiest the desire of every living thing,' Psalm 145:15-16); the produce for the cattle and wild beasts (the bounty reaching the creatures) — the provision (the beasts fed); 'shall all the increase thereof be meat' (the increase as food for the creatures, God's provision for all, the figure, the kindness of God reaching all, Psalm 145:15-16, the providence, the universal provision of the sabbath year) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And for thy cattle, and for the beast that are in thy land, shall all the increase thereof be meat
the sabbath-year produce as food even for the cattle and beasts
the bounty reaching even to the cattle and the wild beasts, God's provision for all his creatures
the kindness of God reaching all that he has made (Psalm 145:15-16)

schools.summary: The numbering of the forty-nine years to the jubilee: 'and thou shalt number seven sabbaths of years unto thee, seven times seven years; and the space of the seven sabbaths of years shall be unto thee forty and nine years' — seven sabbatical periods (seven times seven years) counted, forty-nine years, after which came the jubilee (the seven sabbaths of years, seven seven-year periods, the seven times seven years, the forty-nine years, the figure, the count to the jubilee, the fiftieth year crowning the seven sevens, as the seven weeks to Pentecost crowned the count, Leviticus 23:15-16, so the seven sabbaths of years to the jubilee, the great cycle of release built on the sabbath sevens); the seven sabbatical periods counted (the seven sevens, Leviticus 23:15) — the count (the years to the jubilee); 'the space of the seven sabbaths of years shall be unto thee forty and nine years' (the forty-nine years, the seven sevens complete, the figure, the jubilee crowning the count, Leviticus 23:15-16, the providence, the forty-nine numbered, the jubilee to follow, vv.9-10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt number seven sabbaths of years unto thee, seven times seven years; and the space of the seven sabbaths of years shall be unto thee forty and nine years
the numbering of the forty-nine years to the jubilee
the seven sevens counted, the fiftieth year crowning the count (Leviticus 23:15-16)
the great cycle of release built on the sabbath sevens

schools.summary: The jubilee trumpet sounded on the Day of Atonement: 'then shalt thou cause the trumpet of the jubile to sound on the tenth day of the seventh month, in the day of atonement shall ye make the trumpet sound throughout all your land' — the jubilee proclaimed by the trumpet sounded on the Day of Atonement, the tenth of the seventh month, throughout all the land (the trumpet of the jubile, the ram's horn proclaiming the jubilee, the the tenth day of the seventh month, in the day of atonement, the jubilee beginning on the Day of Atonement, the throughout all your land, the proclamation everywhere, the figure, the jubilee liberty proclaimed on the very Day of Atonement, the release flowing from the atonement, the gospel liberty grounded in the atonement, the trumpet of release sounding from the day of expiation, the great truth that liberty and release come through the atonement, that the year of the Lord's favor follows the day of atoning, Isaiah 61:1-2); the trumpet sounding the jubilee (the proclamation) — the trumpet (the jubilee announced); 'in the day of atonement shall ye make the trumpet sound throughout all your land' (the jubilee beginning on the Day of Atonement, the release flowing from the atonement, Isaiah 61:1-2, the figure, the liberty grounded in the atonement, the providence, the jubilee proclaimed everywhere from the day of atoning) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Then shalt thou cause the trumpet of the jubile to sound on the tenth day of the seventh month, in the day of atonement shall ye make the trumpet sound throughout all your land
the jubilee trumpet sounded on the Day of Atonement
the jubilee liberty proclaimed on the very Day of Atonement, the release flowing from the atonement
the year of the Lord's favor following the day of atoning (Isaiah 61:1-2)

schools.summary: The proclamation of liberty in the jubilee: 'and ye shall hallow the fiftieth year, and proclaim liberty throughout all the land unto all the inhabitants thereof: it shall be a jubile unto you; and ye shall return every man unto his possession, and ye shall return every man unto his family' — the liberty — 'proclaim liberty throughout all the land unto all the inhabitants thereof: it shall be a jubile unto you': the fiftieth year hallowed and liberty proclaimed throughout the land, every man returning to his possession and his family (the witnesses noting the significance — the great release of the jubilee, liberty proclaimed to all, the servant freed, the debtor released, the land returned to its original owner, every man restored to his possession and his family, the lesson — the figure of the gospel liberty, the jubilee a type of the great release that Christ proclaims, 'the Spirit of the Lord is upon me... to preach deliverance to the captives... to preach the acceptable year of the Lord,' Luke 4:18-19, citing Isaiah 61:1-2, the gospel as the true jubilee, the proclamation of liberty to the captives of sin, the release from the bondage of debt and servitude, the restoration of the lost inheritance, the return to the possession and the family, the restoration of all that was lost, a figure of the restoration of our heavenly inheritance and our place in the family of God, the figure — the jubilee as the gospel of liberty and restoration, fulfilled in Christ who proclaims the acceptable year of the Lord, the year of release for the captives of sin, the words 'proclaim liberty throughout all the land' inscribed on the bell of liberty, the enduring witness to the freedom the jubilee proclaims); the fiftieth year hallowed (set apart as the jubilee) — the hallowing (the year of release consecrated); the return to the possession and the family (the restoration of the lost inheritance and place, Luke 4:18-19) — the figure (the restoration in the gospel jubilee, the providence, every man restored) — Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the proclamation of liberty in the jubilee signify?

sides.position: The great release of the jubilee (the servant freed, the debtor released, the inheritance restored) - a figure of the gospel liberty that Christ proclaims, the acceptable year of the Lord, deliverance to the captives of sin (Luke 4:18-19; Isaiah 61:1-2), the restoration of our heavenly inheritance and place in the family of God

themes: And ye shall hallow the fiftieth year, and proclaim liberty throughout all the land unto all the inhabitants thereof: it shall be a jubile unto you
the proclamation of liberty in the jubilee (the crux of the gospel liberty)
the great release of the jubilee, a figure of the gospel liberty Christ proclaims
the acceptable year of the Lord, deliverance to the captives and restoration of the lost inheritance (Luke 4:18-19; Isaiah 61:1-2)

schools.summary: The jubilee year not sown and its produce free: 'a jubile shall that fiftieth year be unto you: ye shall not sow, neither reap that which groweth of itself in it, nor gather the grapes in it of thy vine undressed' — the fiftieth year, the jubilee, like the sabbatical year, not to be sown or its self-grown produce reaped as a harvest (the not sow, the land at rest, as the sabbath year, vv.4-5, the not reap... nor gather, the produce not claimed as a harvest, the figure, the jubilee a year of rest like the sabbatical year, the land free, the produce open to all, the double rest where the forty-ninth, sabbatical, and the fiftieth, jubilee, came together, the great trust in God's provision for two years, vv.21); the jubilee not sown (the land at rest, vv.4) — the rest (the land free); 'neither reap that which groweth of itself in it, nor gather the grapes in it of thy vine undressed' (the produce not claimed, as the sabbath year, vv.5, the figure, the land free, the produce open, the providence, the jubilee rest, the produce eaten from the field, vv.12) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: A jubile shall that fiftieth year be unto you: ye shall not sow, neither reap that which groweth of itself in it, nor gather the grapes in it of thy vine undressed
the jubilee year not sown and its produce free
the jubilee a year of rest like the sabbatical year, the land free (vv.4-5)
the great trust in God's provision

schools.summary: The jubilee holy and its fruit eaten from the field: 'for it is the jubile; it shall be holy unto you: ye shall eat the increase thereof out of the field' — the jubilee was holy, and the people ate the increase directly from the field, not from a stored harvest (the it shall be holy unto you, the jubilee consecrated, set apart, the eat the increase thereof out of the field, the produce eaten from the field as it grew, not gathered into barns, the figure, the holiness of the jubilee, the year consecrated to God, the produce eaten directly from the field, the daily provision, the trust in God's daily supply, 'give us this day our daily bread,' Matthew 6:11, the figure of living from God's open hand, the holy year of release); the jubilee holy (consecrated to God) — the holiness (the year set apart); 'ye shall eat the increase thereof out of the field' (the produce eaten from the field, the daily provision, Matthew 6:11, the figure, living from God's open hand, the providence, the holy jubilee, the daily supply) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: For it is the jubile; it shall be holy unto you: ye shall eat the increase thereof out of the field
the jubilee holy and its fruit eaten from the field
the jubilee consecrated to God, the produce eaten from the field
the trust in God's daily supply (Matthew 6:11)

schools.summary: The return of every man to his possession in the jubilee: 'in the year of this jubile ye shall return every man unto his possession' — in the jubilee every man returned to his possession (his ancestral land, restored to him, the return every man unto his possession, the restoration of the inheritance, the figure, the great restoration of the jubilee, every man's ancestral land returned to him whatever had befallen it, the figure of the restoration of the lost inheritance, the recovery of what was forfeited, a type of the restoration of our heavenly inheritance in Christ, 'an inheritance incorruptible... reserved in heaven for you,' 1 Peter 1:4); the jubilee year (the year of restoration) — the year (of release); 'ye shall return every man unto his possession' (the return to the ancestral land, the inheritance restored, the figure, the restoration of the lost inheritance, 1 Peter 1:4, the providence, every man restored to his possession) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: In the year of this jubile ye shall return every man unto his possession
the return of every man to his possession in the jubilee
the great restoration of the jubilee, the ancestral land returned
a type of the restoration of our heavenly inheritance in Christ (1 Peter 1:4)

schools.summary: The law of just dealing in buying and selling: 'and if thou sell ought unto thy neighbour, or buyest ought of thy neighbour's hand, ye shall not oppress one another' — in buying and selling, the people not to oppress (defraud or overreach) one another (the sell ought... or buyest ought, the dealings of trade, the ye shall not oppress one another, no defrauding or overreaching, the figure, the justice and honesty required in dealings, the love of neighbor in trade, the prohibition of oppression and fraud, 'let no man go beyond and defraud his brother in any matter,' 1 Thessalonians 4:6, the price to be just, reckoned by the years to the jubilee, vv.15-16, the righteousness that does not take advantage of the neighbor); the buying and selling (the dealings of trade) — the dealings (the trade between neighbors); 'ye shall not oppress one another' (no oppression or fraud, the just dealing, 1 Thessalonians 4:6, the figure, the love of neighbor in trade, the providence, the price reckoned justly by the jubilee, vv.15-16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And if thou sell ought unto thy neighbour, or buyest ought of thy neighbour's hand, ye shall not oppress one another
the law of just dealing in buying and selling
the justice and honesty required in dealings, the prohibition of oppression and fraud
the love of neighbor in trade (1 Thessalonians 4:6)

schools.summary: The price reckoned by the years to the jubilee: 'according to the number of years after the jubile thou shalt buy of thy neighbour, and according unto the number of years of the fruits he shall sell unto thee' — the price of land reckoned by the number of years until the next jubilee, when it would return to its owner (the number of years after the jubile, the years remaining until the next jubilee, the according unto the number of years of the fruits, the price by the crops the buyer would gather before the jubilee, the figure, the just price reckoned by the time of possession, the land not sold outright but for the years until the jubilee, the truth that what is bought is really the use for a term, not the land forever, the just valuation that accounts for the jubilee, vv.16); the price by the years to the jubilee (the just reckoning) — the reckoning (the years of possession); 'according unto the number of years of the fruits he shall sell unto thee' (the price by the crops to be gathered, the years of fruits, the figure, the use sold for a term, not the land forever, the providence, the just price by the jubilee, vv.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: According to the number of years after the jubile thou shalt buy of thy neighbour, and according unto the number of years of the fruits he shall sell unto thee
the price reckoned by the years to the jubilee
the just price by the time of possession, the land sold for the years until the jubilee
the use sold for a term, not the land forever

schools.summary: The price rising or falling with the years remaining: 'according to the multitude of years thou shalt increase the price thereof, and according to the fewness of years thou shalt diminish the price of it: for according to the number of the years of the fruits doth he sell unto thee' — the more years until the jubilee, the higher the price (more crops), the fewer years, the lower (fewer crops, the multitude of years... increase the price, more harvests, higher value, the fewness of years... diminish the price, fewer harvests, lower value, the figure, the just proportion of the price to the years of use, the value measured by the harvests to be gathered before the jubilee, the equity that reckons the price by what is truly bought, the use for a term, the honest valuation that neither overcharges nor underpays); the price rising with more years (more harvests) — the proportion (the price by the years); 'according to the fewness of years thou shalt diminish the price of it' (the price falling with fewer years, fewer harvests, the figure, the just proportion, the value by the use, the providence, the equity of the price, the warning against oppression, vv.17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: According to the multitude of years thou shalt increase the price thereof, and according to the fewness of years thou shalt diminish the price of it: for according to the number of the years of the fruits doth he sell unto thee
the price rising or falling with the years remaining
the value measured by the harvests to be gathered, the just proportion
the equity that reckons the price by the use, the honest valuation

schools.summary: The charge to fear God and not oppress one another: 'ye shall not therefore oppress one another; but thou shalt fear thy God: for I am the LORD your God' — the people not to oppress (overreach or defraud) one another, but to fear God, who is the LORD their God (the not oppress one another, no fraud or overreaching, vv.14, the fear thy God, the fear of God the ground of just dealing, the for I am the LORD your God, the seal, the figure, the fear of God as the restraint on injustice, the conscience toward God in dealings with men, the truth that the fear of God guards justice where man cannot see, 'the fear of the LORD is to hate evil,' Proverbs 8:13, the love of neighbor grounded in the fear of God, the dealings done as before God); the prohibition of oppression (vv.14) — the justice (no fraud); 'but thou shalt fear thy God: for I am the LORD your God' (the fear of God the ground of just dealing, the conscience toward God, Proverbs 8:13, the figure, the fear of God guarding justice, the providence, the dealings done before God, the LORD who sees) — JFB, Gill.

themes: Ye shall not therefore oppress one another; but thou shalt fear thy God: for I am the LORD your God
the charge to fear God and not oppress one another
the fear of God as the restraint on injustice, the conscience toward God in dealings
the dealings done as before the LORD who sees (Proverbs 8:13)

schools.summary: The promise of dwelling safely in obedience: 'wherefore ye shall do my statutes, and keep my judgments, and do them; and ye shall dwell in the land in safety' — the people doing God's statutes and judgments would dwell in the land in safety (the do my statutes, and keep my judgments, the obedience required, the ye shall dwell in the land in safety, the promise of security, the figure, the safety that flows from obedience, the security of dwelling under God's blessing, the truth that obedience brings security, the land held in peace under God's care, 'the work of righteousness shall be peace... and assurance for ever,' Isaiah 32:17, the safety of those who keep God's law, the blessing on the obedient); the doing of the statutes and judgments (the obedience) — the obedience (the keeping of the law); 'and ye shall dwell in the land in safety' (the security that flows from obedience, the land held in peace, Isaiah 32:17, the figure, the safety of the obedient, the providence, the land dwelt in safely, the provision promised, vv.19-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Wherefore ye shall do my statutes, and keep my judgments, and do them; and ye shall dwell in the land in safety
the promise of dwelling safely in obedience
the safety that flows from obedience, the security of dwelling under God's blessing
the safety of those who keep God's law (Isaiah 32:17)

schools.summary: The land yielding her fruit and the people fed in safety: 'and the land shall yield her fruit, and ye shall eat your fill, and dwell therein in safety' — the land would yield its fruit, the people eating their fill and dwelling in safety (the the land shall yield her fruit, the fruitfulness under God's blessing, the eat your fill, the abundance, satisfied, the dwell therein in safety, the security, the figure, the fruitfulness and abundance that flow from God's blessing on the obedient, the land yielding her increase, the people satisfied and secure, the promise of plenty and peace to the obedient, 'thou shalt eat the labour of thine hands: happy shalt thou be,' Psalm 128:2, the blessing of fullness and safety under God's care); the land yielding her fruit (the fruitfulness under God's blessing) — the provision (the increase); 'ye shall eat your fill, and dwell therein in safety' (the fullness and security, the people satisfied and safe, Psalm 128:2, the figure, the plenty and peace of the obedient, the providence, the abundance and safety, the trust in God's provision, vv.20-22) — Gill.

themes: And the land shall yield her fruit, and ye shall eat your fill, and dwell therein in safety
the land yielding her fruit and the people fed in safety
the fruitfulness and abundance that flow from God's blessing on the obedient
the people satisfied and secure (Psalm 128:2)

schools.summary: The question of what to eat in the seventh year: 'and if ye shall say, What shall we eat the seventh year? behold, we shall not sow, nor gather in our increase' — God anticipates the anxious question: if the land is not sown in the seventh year, what shall the people eat? (the What shall we eat the seventh year?, the natural anxiety, the question of unbelief or weak faith, the we shall not sow, nor gather in our increase, the apparent want, the land at rest, the figure, the natural fear that questions God's provision, the anxiety that asks how it shall be supplied, the weakness of faith that looks at the seeming want, the question God graciously answers with his promise, vv.21, the care that the gospel rebukes, 'take no thought, saying, What shall we eat?' Matthew 6:31); the anxious question (the fear of want) — the anxiety (the question of weak faith); 'behold, we shall not sow, nor gather in our increase' (the apparent want, the land unsown, the figure, the anxiety that questions God's provision, Matthew 6:31, the providence, the question answered by God's promise, vv.21-22) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And if ye shall say, What shall we eat the seventh year? behold, we shall not sow, nor gather in our increase
the question of what to eat in the seventh year
the natural fear that questions God's provision, the weakness of faith
the question God graciously answers, the care the gospel rebukes (Matthew 6:31)

schools.summary: The promised blessing of the sixth year: 'then I will command my blessing upon you in the sixth year, and it shall bring forth fruit for three years' — the blessing — 'I will command my blessing upon you in the sixth year, and it shall bring forth fruit for three years': God promising to command his blessing on the sixth year so that it would yield enough for three years (the witnesses noting the significance — God's special provision for the year of rest, the sixth year's harvest so blessed as to supply the sixth, the seventh, sabbatical, and into the eighth until the new harvest, the lesson — the God who commands his blessing provides for those who trust and obey, the promise that meets the anxious question of vv.20, the assurance that obedience would not bring want but abundance, God supplying beyond nature for those who rest at his word, the wider truth — the God who feeds his people, who gives more than enough where he commands his blessing, 'my God shall supply all your need according to his riches in glory by Christ Jesus,' Philippians 4:19, the manna doubled before the sabbath a like provision, Exodus 16:5, 22-26, the figure — God's abundant provision for faith and obedience, the blessing commanded that turns the seeming loss of the rest into gain, the trust that obeys and finds God faithful to provide, 'I have been young, and now am old; yet have I not seen the righteous forsaken,' Psalm 37:25); God commanding his blessing on the sixth year (the special provision, Exodus 16:5) — the provision (God's abundant supply); 'and it shall bring forth fruit for three years' (the sixth year yielding for three years, the supply for the sixth, seventh, and eighth, Philippians 4:19, the figure, God's provision beyond nature, the providence, the blessing commanded, the want met) — Calvin, Keil, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the promised blessing of the sixth year teach?

sides.position: God's special provision for the year of rest (the sixth year's harvest supplying three years), the assurance that obedience would not bring want but abundance - the God who supplies all need (Philippians 4:19) and gives more than enough where he commands his blessing, the trust that obeys and finds God faithful

themes: Then I will command my blessing upon you in the sixth year, and it shall bring forth fruit for three years
the promised blessing of the sixth year (the crux of God's provision for faith)
God's special provision for the year of rest, the sixth year's harvest supplying three years
the God who supplies all need where he commands his blessing (Philippians 4:19; Exodus 16:5)

schools.summary: The eating of the old store until the new harvest: 'and ye shall sow the eighth year, and eat yet of old fruit until the ninth year; until her fruits come in ye shall eat of the old store' — in the eighth year they sowed again, yet ate of the old fruit (the abundant sixth-year harvest) until the ninth year's harvest came in (the sow the eighth year, the return to tillage after the sabbath, the eat yet of old fruit until the ninth year, the old store lasting until the new harvest, the until her fruits come in ye shall eat of the old store, the provision bridging to the new, the figure, the abundance of the blessed sixth year lasting through the sabbath and into the ninth, the old store sufficient until the new, the faithfulness of God's provision, the supply that does not fail until the new comes in, the proof of the promised blessing, vv.21); the eating of the old fruit until the ninth year (the abundance lasting, vv.21) — the provision (the old store sufficient); 'until her fruits come in ye shall eat of the old store' (the old store lasting until the new harvest, the provision bridging, the figure, God's faithful supply, the blessing proved, the providence, the abundance sufficient until the new) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And ye shall sow the eighth year, and eat yet of old fruit until the ninth year; until her fruits come in ye shall eat of the old store
the eating of the old store until the new harvest
the abundance of the blessed sixth year lasting through the sabbath and into the ninth
God's faithful supply that does not fail until the new comes in (vv.21)

schools.summary: The land is God's and the people his sojourners: 'the land shall not be sold for ever: for the land is mine; for ye are strangers and sojourners with me' — the land's true owner — 'for the land is mine; for ye are strangers and sojourners with me': the land not to be sold in perpetuity, for it belonged to God, and the people were but strangers and sojourners with him (the witnesses noting the significance — God the true owner of the land, the land his, the people but tenants and pilgrims holding it of him, not absolute owners but stewards, the lesson — the ground of the jubilee and the whole land law, since the land is God's, it cannot be sold forever, but returns to the family he assigned it to, the people hold it of God as tenants, and must deal with it as his, the deeper truth of pilgrimage — 'ye are strangers and sojourners with me,' the people, and all the saints, but pilgrims on the earth, holding all of God, 'we are strangers before thee, and sojourners, as were all our fathers,' 1 Chronicles 29:15, 'I am a stranger with thee, and a sojourner, as all my fathers were,' Psalm 39:12, 'these all died in faith... and confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth,' Hebrews 11:13, 'here have we no continuing city, but we seek one to come,' Hebrews 13:14, the figure — God the owner of all, his people his stewards and pilgrims, holding the land and all things of him for a season, looking for the abiding inheritance, the stewardship that handles all as God's, and the pilgrim heart that holds the world loosely); the land God's, not sold in perpetuity (the true owner, the stewardship) — the ownership (God's title to the land); 'for ye are strangers and sojourners with me' (the people but strangers and sojourners with God, pilgrims holding all of him, 1 Chronicles 29:15; Hebrews 11:13, the figure, the pilgrim heart, the heavenly inheritance, the providence, the land held of God, the jubilee grounded in his ownership) — Calvin, Henry, Wesley, JFB, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'the land is mine; ye are strangers and sojourners with me' teach?

sides.position: God the true owner of all, the people his stewards and pilgrims holding the land of him for a season (1 Chronicles 29:15; Psalm 39:12) - the ground of the jubilee and a figure of the pilgrim life that holds all of God and looks for the abiding inheritance (Hebrews 11:13; 13:14)

themes: The land shall not be sold for ever: for the land is mine; for ye are strangers and sojourners with me
the land is God's and the people his sojourners (the crux of stewardship and pilgrimage)
God the true owner of all, his people his stewards and pilgrims (1 Chronicles 29:15; Psalm 39:12)
a figure of the pilgrim life that holds all of God and looks for the abiding inheritance (Hebrews 11:13; 13:14)

schools.summary: The right of redemption granted for the land: 'and in all the land of your possession ye shall grant a redemption for the land' — throughout the land, a right of redemption granted: land sold could be redeemed (bought back) by the owner or his kinsman before the jubilee (the grant a redemption for the land, the right to redeem, the land never sold beyond recovery, the figure, the right of redemption woven into the land law, the land always redeemable, the figure of redemption, the inheritance never lost beyond recovery, the right that points to the great Redeemer who redeems the lost inheritance, 'in whom we have redemption through his blood,' Ephesians 1:7, the redemption that recovers what was forfeited); the right throughout the land (the redemption everywhere granted) — the extent (the land always redeemable); 'ye shall grant a redemption for the land' (the right to redeem the land, never sold beyond recovery, Ephesians 1:7, the figure, the redemption of the lost inheritance, the great Redeemer, the providence, the land redeemable, the law of redemption following, vv.25-28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And in all the land of your possession ye shall grant a redemption for the land
the right of redemption granted for the land
the land always redeemable, the inheritance never lost beyond recovery
a figure of the great Redeemer who redeems the lost inheritance (Ephesians 1:7)

schools.summary: The kinsman redeeming the brother's land: 'if thy brother be waxen poor, and hath sold away some of his possession, and if any of his kin come to redeem it, then shall he redeem that which his brother sold' — the kinsman-redeemer — 'if any of his kin come to redeem it, then shall he redeem that which his brother sold': if a poor man sold his land, his near kinsman (the goel) might come and redeem it for him, buying it back (the witnesses noting the significance — the kinsman-redeemer, the near relation who had the right and duty to redeem the lost possession of his poor brother, the goel who recovers the forfeited inheritance, the lesson — the figure of Christ the kinsman-Redeemer, the near kinsman who redeems his impoverished brethren, who, being made our brother in the flesh, 'took part of the same,' Hebrews 2:14, comes to redeem the inheritance we lost by sin, the goel who pays the price and recovers our lost estate, the example of Boaz, the kinsman who redeemed the inheritance of Naomi and took Ruth, the near kinsman doing the redeemer's part, Ruth 4:4-10, the figure — Christ our near Kinsman and Redeemer, who, taking our nature, redeems the inheritance forfeited by our poverty in sin, recovering for us the possession we could not keep, 'thou wast slain, and hast redeemed us to God by thy blood,' Revelation 5:9, the redemption of the lost inheritance by the kinsman who has both the right and the love to redeem); the poor brother who sold his possession (the forfeited inheritance) — the poverty (the lost estate); 'if any of his kin come to redeem it, then shall he redeem that which his brother sold' (the kinsman redeeming the brother's land, the goel, the figure of Christ, Ruth 4:4-10; Hebrews 2:14, the figure, the near Kinsman-Redeemer, the providence, the lost inheritance redeemed by the kinsman) — Calvin, Clarke, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

sides.position: The near relation (the goel) who had the right and duty to redeem the lost possession of his poor brother (as Boaz redeemed for Ruth, Ruth 4:4-10) - a figure of Christ our near Kinsman who, taking our nature (Hebrews 2:14), redeems the inheritance forfeited by our poverty in sin, recovering our lost estate by his blood (Revelation 5:9)

themes: If thy brother be waxen poor, and hath sold away some of his possession, and if any of his kin come to redeem it, then shall he redeem that which his brother sold
the kinsman redeeming the brother's land (the crux of the kinsman-redeemer and Christ)
the near kinsman (goel) who recovers the lost inheritance of his poor brother (Ruth 4:4-10)
a figure of Christ our near Kinsman who redeems the inheritance forfeited by our sin (Hebrews 2:14; Revelation 5:9)

schools.summary: The man who redeems his own land: 'and if the man have none to redeem it, and himself be able to redeem it' — if the poor man had no kinsman to redeem his land, but himself became able, he might redeem it (the have none to redeem it, no kinsman to act the redeemer, the himself be able to redeem it, the man himself recovering means to buy it back, the figure, the provision that the land might be recovered by the man himself if no kinsman redeemed, the right of redemption not lost even where no kinsman acts, the inheritance recoverable, the figure of the recovery of what was lost, the door of redemption open, vv.27); the man without a kinsman-redeemer (no near kin to act) — the lack (no redeemer at hand); 'and himself be able to redeem it' (the man himself recovering means to redeem, the inheritance recoverable, the figure, the right of redemption open even without a kinsman, the providence, the man redeeming his own land, vv.27) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And if the man have none to redeem it, and himself be able to redeem it
the man who redeems his own land
the land recoverable by the man himself if no kinsman redeemed
the right of redemption open, the inheritance recoverable

schools.summary: The reckoning of the redemption price: 'then let him count the years of the sale thereof, and restore the overplus unto the man to whom he sold it; that he may return unto his possession' — in redeeming, the man counted the years since the sale, and paid back the value of the years remaining until the jubilee (the overplus), and so returned to his possession (the count the years of the sale, the years since it was sold, the restore the overplus, pay the value of the remaining years to the buyer, the that he may return unto his possession, the inheritance recovered, the figure, the just reckoning of the redemption price, the value of the remaining years repaid, the figure of the price paid to redeem the inheritance, the recovery of the possession by the redemption price, a shadow of the price Christ paid to redeem us, 'ye were not redeemed with corruptible things... but with the precious blood of Christ,' 1 Peter 1:18-19); the reckoning of the years and the repaying of the overplus (the just price) — the reckoning (the redemption price); 'that he may return unto his possession' (the inheritance recovered by the price paid, 1 Peter 1:18-19, the figure, the price of redemption, Christ's blood, the providence, the possession restored by redemption) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Then let him count the years of the sale thereof, and restore the overplus unto the man to whom he sold it; that he may return unto his possession
the reckoning of the redemption price
the value of the remaining years repaid, the inheritance recovered by the redemption price
a shadow of the price Christ paid to redeem us (1 Peter 1:18-19)

schools.summary: The inheritance restored at the jubilee if not redeemed: 'but if he be not able to restore it to him, then that which is sold shall remain in the hand of him that hath bought it until the year of jubile: and in the jubile it shall go out, and he shall return unto his possession' — if the man could not redeem the land, it remained with the buyer until the jubilee, when it returned freely to the original owner (the not able to restore it, no means to redeem, the remain... until the year of jubile, the land held by the buyer till the jubilee, the in the jubile it shall go out... he shall return unto his possession, the free restoration at the jubilee, the figure, the jubilee restoring the inheritance freely even where redemption failed, the grace that restores what could not be redeemed by the man himself, the figure of the free restoration of grace, the inheritance recovered at last by the jubilee whether or not the man could redeem it, the certain restoration that the jubilee guaranteed, the grace that gives back what was lost); the land held by the buyer until the jubilee (where redemption failed) — the holding (until the jubilee); 'in the jubile it shall go out, and he shall return unto his possession' (the free restoration at the jubilee, the inheritance returned, the figure, the grace that restores what could not be redeemed, the providence, the certain restoration of the jubilee) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: But if he be not able to restore it to him, then that which is sold shall remain in the hand of him that hath bought it until the year of jubile: and in the jubile it shall go out, and he shall return unto his possession
the inheritance restored at the jubilee if not redeemed
the jubilee restoring the inheritance freely even where redemption failed
the grace that gives back what was lost, the certain restoration the jubilee guaranteed

schools.summary: Gill: the walled city was such from the days of Joshua; the house there might be redeemed within a whole year.
Keil & Delitzsch: on the sale of a dwelling-house in a walled town, the right of redemption or repurchase was retained for a definite period, a full year from the sale.

themes: if a man sell a dwelling house in a walled city
within a full year may he redeem it
redemption left open even for the city house

schools.summary: Gill: the failure to redeem might be by the seller or any of his kin; the house then was established to the buyer.
Keil & Delitzsch: if not redeemed within a full year, the house in the walled city was established for ever to the buyer throughout his generations, and did not go out in the jubilee.

themes: established for ever to him that bought it
it shall not go out in the jubile
the land the true inheritance God guarded

schools.summary: Gill: such villages had no walls in the days of Joshua, even if afterward walled; their houses counted as fields.
Ellicott: houses in villages are part of the landed property, and like the cultivated land are subject to the law of jubilee.
Wesley: the village houses belonged to and were necessary for the management of the lands.

themes: the houses of the villages which have no wall
counted as the fields... go out in the jubile
the farmer's homestead never permanently lost

schools.summary: Gill: this excepts the Levites' own cities — the cities of refuge and the forty-two others — not such as they might purchase elsewhere; these they might redeem at any time.
Keil & Delitzsch: for the Levites there was eternal redemption — when their houses were sold, the right of repurchase was never lost.

themes: the cities of the Levites... may the Levites redeem at any time
a perpetual right of redemption
God himself the Levites' portion

schools.summary: Gill, with Jarchi: if a common Israelite purchased, it was redeemable and returned at jubilee, but if a Levite bought of a Levite it was irredeemable.
Keil & Delitzsch: the house sold went out in the year of jubilee, for the houses of the cities of the Levites are their possession among the children of Israel.

themes: if a man purchase of the Levites... shall go out in the year of jubile
the houses... are their possession
the Levites' inheritance held secure

schools.summary: Gill: the suburbs reached two thousand cubits on every side, held in common, no Levite having a field of his own; so it might not be sold lest they lose their pasture; the Jewish writers also understood it of not changing field, suburb, and city one for another.

themes: the field of the suburbs... may not be sold
for it is their perpetual possession
the shared livelihood of the tribe protected

schools.summary: Gill: the brother is an Israelite reduced to a low estate through affliction in body or family, losses in trade, or want of business.
Keil & Delitzsch: the relief extended even to the stranger or sojourner, that the impoverished brother might live with him.

themes: if thy brother be waxen poor... then thou shalt relieve him
though he be a stranger, or a sojourner
God's people the support of the poor

schools.summary: Gill: one should not only relieve the brother for the present but lend him money to set him up in business for the future, without requiring interest.
Keil & Delitzsch: neither interest on money nor an addition on food was to be demanded (cf. Exod. 22:24; Deut. 23:20-21), from fear of God who had redeemed Israel from bondage.

themes: Take thou no usury of him, or increase
but fear thy God
no gain from a brother's distress

schools.summary: Gill: this is of persons in poor and needy circumstances; otherwise, if one borrows to gain by trade or purchase, it is reasonable the lender share the profit.
Keil & Delitzsch: not to give money on usury nor lend victuals for increase, grounded in the LORD who brought Israel from Egypt (v.38).

debates.question: Does this forbid all interest, or only usury of the poor?

sides.position: Only the lending at interest to the poor and distressed is forbidden; fair gain in commerce is left
The law as given simply forbids interest and increase from the brother

themes: not give him thy money upon usury, nor... victuals for increase
the text speaking of the needy
the poor guarded, fair commerce left

schools.summary: Gill: having been strangers in Egypt and there shown favour, and brought out by God, they should be kind to the needy and lend freely; this is the preface to the commandments.
Ellicott: the LORD who brought them out of Egypt to give them Canaan and to be their God is the ground of the law of charity.

themes: I am the LORD your God, which brought you forth out of... Egypt
to give you the land of Canaan
redemption the spring of Israel's mercy

schools.summary: Gill: the foregoing laws were meant to prevent such extreme poverty, but where relief failed, the brother might be sold; yet not held as a bondservant.
Henry: the laws of servitude were designed to preserve the honour of the Jewish nation as a free people, rescued by divine power out of the house of bondage into the glorious liberty of God's people.

themes: if thy brother... be sold unto thee; thou shalt not compel him to serve as a bondservant
Israel a free people redeemed from bondage
the poor brother keeping his dignity

schools.summary: Gill: the hired servant serves for a time, receives his wages, and is not under the despotic power of a slave.

Ellicott: the master must treat him as one who disposes of his service for wages for a time, and could not keep him beyond the year of jubilee, which terminated the sale of his services as of landed property.

themes: as an hired servant... he shall be with thee
shall serve thee unto the year of jubile
liberty the standing condition, bondage passing

schools.summary: Gill: his wife and children went out with him, whom the master was bound to maintain though they were not his servants.
Ellicott: at the same time he regained his liberty and took his family, the patrimony he had sold also reverted to him.

themes: then shall he depart... both he and his children
return unto... the possession of his fathers
the jubilee a complete restoration by grace

schools.summary: Gill: the Lord redeemed them out of Egypt, made a purchase of them, and had the prior right; being his servants first, they could not be the servants of others.
Clarke: redeemed from Egyptian bondage, they were God's property and should not alienate themselves from him; in being his servants both their religious and political service consisted.

themes: they are my servants, which I brought forth out of... Egypt
they shall not be sold as bondmen
Israel's freedom flowing from belonging to God

schools.summary: Gill: the word recalls how the Egyptians ruled Israel with rigour, that they might abstain from such usage of their brethren — tyranny, oppression, and unbearable burdens.
Keil & Delitzsch: thou shalt not rule over him with rigour, but fear thy God.

themes: Thou shalt not rule over him with rigour
but shalt fear thy God
the memory of Egypt the guard of mercy

schools.summary: Gill: this answers the objection — if a brother may not be a bondman, who shall minister to me? — by allowing servants of other nations.
Ellicott: since the law forbade the Israelites to have their brethren as bondmen, the bondmen they must have were to be of the heathen round about.

themes: thy bondmen, and thy bondmaids... shall be of the heathen
of them shall ye buy
the covenant brotherhood not reduced to slavery

schools.summary: Gill: these are the uncircumcised sojourners, proselytes of the gate, who lived among Israel subject to the Noahic precepts but had not embraced the Jewish religion.
Keil & Delitzsch: of the children of the strangers that sojourn among them, and of their families begotten in the land, they might buy and hold them as a possession.

themes: of the children of the strangers that do sojourn among you... shall ye buy
they shall be your possession
the distinction from the covenant brother kept

schools.summary: Gill: such servants might be left at death to the children to inherit, like fields and vineyards, as immovable goods.

Ellicott: they might be appropriated as personal property, transmissible as inheritance, not subject to the laws of jubilee, in perpetual serfdom unless redeemed; but the brethren were not to be ruled with rigour.

themes: take them as an inheritance for your children... bondmen for ever
over your brethren... not... with rigour
the covenant kindred under the law of brotherhood

schools.summary: Gill: the stranger, an uncircumcised sojourner, might by trading with Israel grow rich enough to buy a Hebrew servant, though not his lands.
Ellicott: a stranger who has taken up his abode among the Israelites and become prosperous by trade; the impoverished brother sells himself to him.

themes: if a sojourner or stranger wax rich... and thy brother... sell himself unto the stranger
a son of the covenant under a heathen hand
the case calling for urgent rescue

schools.summary: Gill: unlike a heathen sold to an Israelite (a bondman for ever), an Israelite sold to a heathen might be redeemed; the Jewish writers held the kin obliged to redeem him, lest he be polluted with idolatry or swallowed up among the heathen.

themes: After that he is sold he may be redeemed again
one of his brethren may redeem him
the kinsman-redeemer, shadow of Christ (1 Pet. 1:18-19)

schools.summary: Gill: the kin is the father's brother or his father's brother's son.

Ellicott: any of his relatives are to redeem him — not the case when an Israelite sells himself to his own nation; the Jewish canons bound even the Sanhedrin to compel the kin to redeem him, lest he be lost among the heathen.

themes: Either his uncle, or his uncle's son, may redeem him
or... he may redeem himself
the law multiplying the hands that set him free

schools.summary: Gill: either the man himself or whoever undertook to redeem him should reckon with the buyer.

Clarke: a very equitable law — the years to the jubilee were counted, and the current yearly wage of a servant set against them, that the redemption be no prejudice to the master nor a wrong to the Israelite.

themes: he shall reckon with him that bought him... unto the year of jubile
the price... according unto the number of years
justice even between redeemer and owner

schools.summary: Gill: 'many years behind' means more years to the year of jubilee than he had already served; according to them he gives the greater price of his redemption out of the money he was bought for.

themes: If there be yet many years behind
according unto them he shall give... the price of his redemption
the price exact to the years recovered

schools.summary: Gill: if he has served fifteen years and but five remain to the jubilee, the less is given for his redemption.
Keil & Delitzsch: if but few years remain, the price is counted according to his years, the buyer keeping him meanwhile as a hired servant.

themes: if there remain but few years unto the year of jubile
according unto his years shall he give... the price
the structure leaning toward liberty

schools.summary: Gill: being redeemable every year, he was to be valued and treated in a kind and gentle manner, as a hired servant, not a bondman.
Ellicott: as long as he is in service his master must not treat him like a slave, but as a yearly hired servant.

themes: as a yearly hired servant shall he be with him
shall not rule with rigour over him in thy sight
the community watching over its own

schools.summary: Gill: the text may be filled 'in these years' or 'by these' means — by his kin or himself; if neither redeemed him, the jubilee freed him.
Keil & Delitzsch: if not redeemed, he went out in the year of jubilee, both he and his children with him.

themes: if he be not redeemed in these years... he shall go out in the year of jubile
both he, and his children
the jubilee reaching into every bondage

schools.summary: Gill: as those redeemed by the blood of Christ should not be perpetual servants of men, so Israel, God's servants whom he brought out of Egypt, were not to be enslaved to others, but laid under obligation to serve the LORD, who would not have others rule over them with rigour; the redeemed are bought with a price, not to be the servants of men (1 Cor. 7:23).

themes: unto me the children of Israel are servants
whom I brought forth out of... Egypt
the redeemed bought with a price (1 Cor. 7:23)
